metaTitle: Divorce Process in Bangladesh 2026 | Complete Legal Guide
title: Divorce Process in Bangladesh 2026: Complete Guide for Muslim, Hindu & Court Divorce
metaDescription: A complete guide to the divorce process in Bangladesh in 2026 — covering Muslim talaq/khula, Hindu dissolution, court procedures, alimony, and custody.
keywords: how to get divorced in Bangladesh
heroIntro: Divorce in Bangladesh is governed by distinct personal laws depending on religious community — Muslim, Hindu, or Christian. Understanding the correct legal process is essential to protecting your rights regarding dower, child custody, alimony, and property. This complete 2026 guide walks you through every step of the Bangladesh divorce process.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Divorce Process in Bangladesh
points: Muslim divorce requires notice to the local Arbitration Council (Union Parishad or City Corporation)
Talaq divorce takes 90 days; khula (wife-initiated) requires court or mutual consent
Hindu divorce has limited grounds under customary law and requires court proceedings
Family court handles custody, dower, alimony — governed by Family Courts Ordinance 1985
A divorce lawyer helps ensure proper notice, arbitration, and protection of your rights

sections.h2: Legal Framework: Which Law Applies to Your Divorce?
sections.content:
Bangladesh applies a system of personal laws where the applicable divorce law depends on the religion of the parties involved:

  • Muslims: The Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 and Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939
  • Hindus: Customary personal law (no codified divorce statute for Hindus in Bangladesh, but courts recognize certain grounds)
  • Christians: The Divorce Act 1869
  • Special Marriage: The Special Marriage Act 1872 for inter-faith or civil marriages

For all communities, procedural aspects (court process, filing, evidence) are governed by the Code of Civil Procedure 1908 and the Family Courts Ordinance 1985. A qualified family lawyer in Bangladesh can advise which law applies to your specific situation.

sections.h2: Muslim Divorce: Talaq, Khula & Mubaarat Explained
sections.content:
Muslim divorce in Bangladesh can take three main forms:

1. Talaq (Husband-Initiated Divorce): The husband pronounces divorce by written notice sent to both the wife and the Chairman of the relevant Union Parishad or City Corporation Ward Councilor (the Arbitration Council). Talaq becomes effective only after 90 days — during which an arbitration council attempts reconciliation.

2. Khula (Wife-Initiated Divorce): The wife seeks divorce by relinquishing her dower (mahr) or other agreed consideration. If the husband agrees, the arbitration council processes the mutual divorce. If the husband does not consent, the wife must apply to the Family Court for judicial khula.

3. Mubaarat (Mutual Divorce): Both parties mutually agree to dissolve the marriage. The process mirrors talaq but is faster since both parties cooperate. Notice to the Arbitration Council is still required, and the 90-day reconciliation period applies.

Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939: A Muslim wife can also obtain a judicial divorce (faskh) on grounds including: husband's unknown whereabouts for 4+ years, failure to maintain for 2+ years, husband's imprisonment for 7+ years, cruelty, impotence, or other grounds.

sections.h2: The Arbitration Council Procedure for Muslim Divorce
sections.content:
The Arbitration Council is a mandatory step in Muslim divorce under the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961. Here is the step-by-step process:

  1. Issue Divorce Notice: The divorcing party (husband for talaq, wife for khula) sends a written divorce notice to the Chairman of the Union Parishad, City Corporation Ward, or Paurashava office where the marriage was registered or where the parties last resided.
  2. Copy to Spouse: A copy of the divorce notice must be served on the other spouse simultaneously.
  3. Arbitration Council Formation: Within 30 days of receiving the notice, the Chairman constitutes an Arbitration Council with one representative from each side and the Chairman as head.
  4. Reconciliation Attempt: The council meets to attempt reconciliation. Attendance is important — if either party fails to cooperate, the council proceeds without them.
  5. 90-Day Waiting Period: If reconciliation fails, the divorce becomes effective after 90 days from the date the Chairman received the original notice (or after the wife's iddat period concludes if she is pregnant).
  6. Divorce Certificate: After the 90-day period, obtain a formal divorce certificate from the Chairman's office for legal and administrative purposes.

sections.h2: Hindu Divorce in Bangladesh: Legal Options
sections.content:
Unlike India, Bangladesh does not have a codified Hindu divorce law (no equivalent of India's Hindu Marriage Act 1955). For Bangladeshi Hindus, divorce is extremely restricted and is governed primarily by customary law and limited judicial precedent.

Courts in Bangladesh have recognized divorce among Hindus in limited circumstances, including:

  • Conversion of one spouse to another religion
  • Customary divorce recognized in certain communities
  • Bigamy or abandonment in specific cases

In practice, many Hindu couples in Bangladesh who wish to separate face significant legal obstacles. Legal separation, maintenance orders, and custody arrangements are possible even without a formal divorce decree.

Hindu women can also seek maintenance under Section 488 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. Consulting an experienced family law attorney in Dhaka is critical for navigating the limited options available.

sections.h2: Christian Divorce Under the Divorce Act 1869
sections.content:
Christians in Bangladesh divorce under the Divorce Act 1869. The grounds for divorce include:

  • Adultery (historically the primary ground)
  • Conversion to another religion
  • Unsound mind for 2+ years
  • Incurable leprosy or venereal disease
  • Desertion for 2+ years
  • Cruelty

Christian divorce proceedings are conducted in the Family Court or Civil Court with appropriate jurisdiction. The court may also order judicial separation instead of full divorce. Alimony and child custody arrangements follow general family court principles under the Family Courts Ordinance 1985.

sections.h2: Family Court: Custody, Dower & Alimony Claims
sections.content:
The Family Courts Ordinance 1985 establishes Family Courts with exclusive jurisdiction over:

  • Dissolution of marriage (for khula, faskh, or contested divorces)
  • Restitution of conjugal rights
  • Dower (mahr): A Muslim wife's right to receive the agreed dower amount as specified in the Kabinnama (marriage contract)
  • Maintenance: The court can order monthly maintenance for a wife and children during and after divorce proceedings
  • Custody and guardianship of minor children

Child Custody in Bangladesh: Under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890, courts prioritize the 'welfare of the child.' Generally, children below 7 years (boys) and girls until puberty remain with the mother, but all decisions are made on a case-by-case basis considering the child's best interests.

Alimony/Maintenance: Courts consider the husband's income, standard of living during marriage, and the wife's own financial resources when determining maintenance amounts.

sections.h2: Step-by-Step: Filing for Divorce in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Here is a simplified step-by-step overview of filing for divorce in Bangladesh:

  1. Consult a Lawyer: Engage a qualified divorce lawyer in Dhaka to assess your situation and advise on the correct legal process.
  2. Gather Documents: Collect your Kabinnama (marriage certificate), national ID, birth certificates of children, proof of address, and any evidence relevant to your case.
  3. Issue Divorce Notice (Muslim cases): Draft and serve divorce notice on the Arbitration Council Chairman and the spouse.
  4. Attend Arbitration: Participate in the arbitration council proceedings during the 90-day reconciliation period.
  5. File Court Petition (if needed): For contested matters — khula, maintenance, custody, dower — file a petition in the relevant Family Court.
  6. Serve Summons: The court issues summons to the other party, who must file a written response.
  7. Evidence and Hearing: Both parties present evidence. The court may attempt conciliation before proceeding to full trial.
  8. Obtain Decree: The court issues a divorce decree. Collect the certified copy for administrative use.

sections.h2: Timeline and Costs of Divorce in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Timeline:

  • Mutual/uncontested talaq or mubaarat: 90 days minimum through arbitration council
  • Uncontested Family Court divorce: 6–12 months
  • Contested divorce with custody/property disputes: 1–4 years
  • Appeals to High Court Division: Add 1–3 additional years

Approximate Costs (2026):

  • Lawyer fee for basic uncontested divorce: BDT 20,000–60,000
  • Lawyer fee for contested divorce: BDT 1,00,000–5,00,000+
  • Court filing fees and stamp duty: BDT 2,000–10,000 depending on claim amounts
  • Arbitration council fees: Nominal (set by local government)

These figures are approximate and vary by case complexity, lawyer seniority, and jurisdiction. Contact our divorce law team for a case-specific consultation.

faqs.question: Can a wife divorce her husband in Bangladesh without his consent?
faqs.answer: Yes. A Muslim wife can file for judicial khula in the Family Court without the husband's consent, or seek a decree of divorce under the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939 on specific grounds such as cruelty, failure to maintain, or unknown whereabouts for 4+ years.

faqs.question: What is iddat and how does it affect divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Iddat is the mandatory waiting period a Muslim woman must observe after divorce before she can remarry. It is typically three menstrual cycles (approximately 3 months) or until childbirth if she is pregnant. Divorce does not become legally complete until after the iddat period.

faqs.question: Is divorce valid if not registered in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: For Muslim divorce, proper notice to the Arbitration Council is legally required. Without it, the divorce may be legally challenged. Registration provides essential documentary evidence for administrative purposes (passport, remarriage, inheritance claims).

faqs.question: Who gets custody of children after divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Courts apply the 'welfare of the child' principle under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890. Generally, young children stay with the mother, but fathers retain visitation rights. For older children, courts consider the child's own preference, financial capability of each parent, and overall welfare.

faqs.question: Can I get a divorce if my spouse is abroad or cannot be found?
faqs.answer: Yes. For Muslim husbands, a wife can seek divorce on grounds of the husband's absence for 4+ years. Courts can issue divorce decrees even in the absence of the other party through substituted service of summons (service by publication or other means).
